description: Rendaku is a Japanese morphophonological process where the initial voiceless consonant of the second element of a compound word becomes voiced. The most common pattern is /t/ → [d], but it also includes /k/ → [ɡ], /s/ → [z], and /h/ → [b]. This is a systematic sandhi rule triggered by morphological composition.
sources: Vance, T. (1987). _An Introduction to Japanese Phonology_. SUNY Press.
Ito, J., & Mester, A. (1999). _The Phonology of Japanese_. In _The Handbook of Phonological Theory_. Blackwell.
K intr, D. (2015). _Rendaku: A morphophonological conspiracy_. In _Japanese/Korean Linguistics_ (Vol. 22, pp. 265-279). Linguistic Society of America.
